text: The surgeon examines a parastomal and midline hernia defect in a patient with a previous ilioconduit and radical cystectomy. The incarcerated small bowel is visible along the viscera, while the remaining hernia contents consist of omentum, which is being removed from the midline defect. The surgical field includes a left-sided dock with three ports. The conduit is visible, highlighted by ICG introduced via a Foley catheter, although there are minimal adhesions present.

The surgeon prepares to place a large piece of mesh over the anterior abdominal wall by taking down the falciform ligament, ensuring there is no fatty tissue between the mesh and the abdominal wall. The catheter is removed, and the mesh is positioned without tension, allowing for lateralization. The midline defect is closed with reduced pressure, showing no tension during the closure.
The surgeon cinches the mesh down tightly over the abdominal wall, ensuring a secure fit, and proceeds to close the peristomal defect.

The surgeon measures the space and selects a 20x25 coated mesh for the hernia repair. The mesh is positioned laparoscopically with a few tacks to hold it in place, marking the midline and areas around the urostomy. The surgeon prefers suturing over tacking, and redocks to continue the procedure, using two trans-facial sutures on the lateral aspect of the stoma.

The surgeon sews the mesh into place circumferentially, creating a mesh tunnel around the conduit by placing sutures close to the bowel to minimize friction. Suturing begins on the cephalad side of the stoma, with two additional sutures completing the perimeter around the mesh.
